content.text: display and position are the two CSS properties that control whether an element participates in layout, how it interacts with surrounding elements, and where it is physically placed on the page. Mastering them is the foundation of every layout pattern in CSS.

attrs.alt: CSS Display & Positioning — values, stacking context, containing block, visibility comparison

content.text: The display Property

display sets the type of rendering box an element produces. It controls two things simultaneously: how the element behaves in relation to its parent and siblings (its outer display type), and how its children are laid out (its inner display type).
block

Block elements start on a new line and stretch to fill the full width of their container. Vertical stacking is the natural flow. Most layout elements —
,

,

–

,
,
— are block by default.
inline
Inline elements flow alongside text, like words in a sentence. They do not start a new line. Width, height, and vertical margins have no effect — only horizontal padding and margin apply visually.
inline-block

inline-block is a hybrid: the element flows inline (does not start a new line) but respects width, height, and all four sides of margin and padding. It is useful for button-like elements and inline chips:
flex and grid

flex and grid change how the element's children are laid out. The element itself is still block-level. These are covered in depth in their own sections; the key point here is that setting display: flex or display: grid creates a new formatting context for all direct children.
none
display: none removes the element completely from the layout and the accessibility tree. It takes no space. Screen readers do not announce it, and it cannot receive keyboard focus.

.modal { display: none; }
.modal.is-open { display: block; }  /* Angular: [class.is-open]="isOpen()" */
contents
display: contents makes the element itself disappear from the layout box tree, but keeps its children. This is useful for semantic wrapper elements that should not create a layout container:
The position Property

position controls how an element is placed in the document and whether it is removed from the normal flow. Elements that are removed from flow — absolute and fixed — do not occupy space in the document layout.
static (default)
Every element starts as position: static. Offset properties (top, right, bottom, left, inset) have no effect. z-index has no effect. The element follows the normal document flow.
relative

position: relative keeps the element in the normal flow — it still occupies its original space — but allows it to be visually offset from that position using top, right, bottom, and left. It also establishes a new containing block for absolutely-positioned children.
absolute

position: absolute removes the element from normal flow (it takes no space). It is positioned relative to its nearest ancestor that has position other than static — its containing block. If no such ancestor exists, it positions relative to the initial containing block (viewport).
Always add position: relative to the intended parent when using absolute positioning on a child. Without it, the child positions against a distant ancestor.
fixed

position: fixed removes the element from flow and positions it relative to the viewport. It stays in the same screen position as the user scrolls — the defining behavior for navbars, modals, and toast notifications.

Important: if any ancestor element has a transform, filter, perspective, or will-change: transform applied, position: fixed behaves like position: absolute — it positions relative to that ancestor, not the viewport.
sticky
position: sticky is a hybrid between relative and fixed. The element behaves as relative until it reaches its threshold (top, left, etc.) within its scroll container, at which point it sticks.
The element only sticks within its parent's scroll area — when the parent scrolls off, the element goes with it
overflow: hidden or overflow: auto on any ancestor prevents sticky from working — it must be overflow: visible
Sticky is perfect for table headers, section headers in long lists, and sidebar navigation
The inset Shorthand
The inset property is a shorthand for top, right, bottom, and left. It accepts the same 1–4 value syntax as margin/padding:
Stacking Context and z-index
z-index controls the front-to-back order of overlapping elements. It only works on positioned elements (any position value other than static). A higher z-index appears in front.
A stacking context is an isolated z-index environment. Elements inside a stacking context compete only with each other for z-order — they cannot be interleaved with elements outside their context.
Properties that create a new stacking context:
position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky + any z-index other than auto
opacity less than 1 (opacity: 0.99 creates a context)
transform, filter, perspective with any value other than none
will-change: transform (or other paint-related properties)
isolation: isolate (explicitly creates a context without side effects)
Stacking context is the most common source of z-index bugs. If a high z-index element is not appearing on top, check whether its ancestor creates a stacking context that is below the competitor.

Angular: Toggling Display and Visibility
Angular provides built-in structural directives and signal-based patterns that map cleanly to display/visibility strategies:

@if (isMenuOpen()) {
...
}
Loading...
{{ tooltipText() }}
...
@if is equivalent to display: none — the element is removed from the DOM and accessibility tree
[hidden] maps to HTML's hidden attribute, which browsers style as display: none
Class binding with CSS transitions gives the smoothest UX — the class triggers CSS animations
cdkTrapFocus (from @angular/cdk/a11y) should be combined with position: fixed modals for keyboard focus management
Common Positioning Patterns
Centered Modal
Sticky Sidebar
Badge on Card
Common Pitfalls
z-index on a static element — has no effect; add position: relative to activate it
Absolute child without relative parent — positions against the wrong ancestor
overflow: hidden preventing sticky — sticky requires overflow: visible on all ancestors
Fixed element shifted by a transformed ancestor — any ancestor with transform creates a new containing block for fixed descendants
display: none in animations — cannot be transitioned; use opacity/visibility combo instead
Angular :host with no display — custom elements are inline by default; always set display: block or flex on :host
float and clear — Text Wrapping
Before Flexbox and Grid, float was the primary layout tool. Today its only legitimate use is for the original purpose it was designed for: wrapping text around an image.
Floated elements are removed from normal flow, which causes their parent container to collapse (zero height). Two modern solutions for containing floats:
Do not use float for multi-column layouts — use Flexbox or Grid instead
Float is still correct for magazine-style text-wraps around inline images
display: flow-root is the preferred container fix — it avoids the overflow: hidden clipping side effect
overflow and Positioning Interaction
overflow affects layout in several ways that interact with positioning:
overflow value
Position interaction
overflow: visible (default)
Positioned children can visually escape the container
overflow: hidden
Clips absolutely-positioned children to the box boundary
overflow: auto/scroll
Creates a scroll container; fixed children position relative to it (not viewport)
overflow: hidden on ancestor
Breaks position: sticky — sticky needs overflow: visible on all scrolling ancestors

overflow: hidden on a container will clip absolutely-positioned children that overflow its boundary. If a badge or tooltip is getting clipped, move the containing context (position: relative) to a parent that is NOT overflow: hidden.
Summary

display controls whether an element is block (full-row, stacks), inline (flows with text, no box sizing), inline-block (inline flow but box model applies), flex/grid (layout container), or none (removed entirely). position: static is the default; relative offsets without removing from flow; absolute and fixed remove from flow and position against a containing block or viewport; sticky blends both behaviors. z-index only works on non-static elements and is scoped to stacking contexts — elements cannot escape their stacking context's z-order. In Angular, @if removes elements from DOM (like display: none), while class bindings enable CSS-driven transitions that use opacity and visibility.
